texts.orig: The energy required for water desalination is rising. Desalination plants  currently  consume  approximately  75.2  TWh  of  electricity  per year,  which  equates  to  0.4%  of  global  electricity  consumption  and would result in 218 million tons of CO2 emissions annually by 2040 [1]. Hence, improving energy efficiency of desalination technologies is an important path for decarbonization. Among various desalination technologies, reverse osmosis (RO) is the most energy-efficient technology with specific electricity consumption (SEC) of 2.5 -4.0 kWh/m 3 , which is very  close  to  its  thermodynamic  limit  [2 -4];  however,  currently commercialized membranes are limited to treating saline water of up to 70,000 ppm in total dissolved solids (TDS) that corresponds to the hydraulic pressure (80 bar) [5,6]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation (MD) is a promising candidate for desalinating hypersaline brine, but its poor energy efficiency  has remained a major barrier for widespread application. One possible solution to this issue is to recover the latent heat in the process. In this work, a multi-stage vacuum MD (MSVMD) was integrated with a mechanical vapor compressor (MVC) to enhance the latent heat recovery, and the energetic and exergetic performance of this integrated process was examined. A comprehensive energy and exergy analysis is provided to compare MSVMD and MSVMD-MVC processes for desalination of hypersaline brine. This analysis was conducted by examining the effect of the compression ratio on the energetic and exergetic performance, and the findings are reported in terms of specific thermal energy consumption (STEC), specific electricity consumption (SEC), and exergetic  efficiency.  The  energy  analysis  shows  that  thermal  energy  consumption  can  be  reduced  as  the compression ratio increases, due to the enhancement of latent heat recovery. The MSVMD-MVC process can be operated in a steady-state condition, without the need for thermal heat input; with STEC and SEC of 0 and 49 kWh/m 3 at  the  feed  temperature  of  50 ◦ C  and  MVC  compression  ratio  of  2.14.  Moreover,  exergy  analysis demonstrates the efficacy of the eNRTL model in exergy calculation. Exergy destruction can be greatly reduced by increasing the compression ratio to an optimal value. For high salinity brine (124 g/L), MSVMD-MVC achieved  a  higher  exergetic  efficiency  of  6.85%,  compared  to  2.42%  in  MSVMD.  The  result  suggests  that  the application of MVC can intensify the energy efficiency and exergetic efficiency of the MSVMD system, although this process cannot outperform the current desalination technologies from the standard primary energy point of view.

texts.orig: among which membrane distillation (MD) is considered to be a promising candidate. MD is a thermally driven desalination process in which water  evaporates  at  the  membrane  feed  side  and  diffuses  through  a hydrophobic  microporous  membrane  due  to  a  transmembrane  vapor pressure difference. The membrane repels liquid water while allowing water vapor to diffuse  through  the  membrane  pores,  which  leads  to 100% theoretical rejection of salts [7]. The hydrophobic nature of MD allows to recover the majority of water and valuable mineral salts while reducing  the  volume  of  concentrated  brine  disposal  that  constitutes environmental pollution [6]. Since evaporation in MD takes place at operating temperatures lower than the normal boiling point, solar energy or waste heat can be employed to offset a portion of the energy requirements for MD [8]. A major barrier to this technology is, however, MD ' s low energy efficiency.

texts.orig: MD (SGMD), and vacuum MD (VMD) [9]. DCMD is the most studied one because of its simple configuration in which feed and permeate flows are both in direct contact with the membrane. DCMD can be less energyintensive  due  to  ease  of  heat  recovery  if  integrated  with  a  heat exchanger;  however,  in  the  case  of  VMD,  recovering  latent  heat  of condensation appears to be difficult and consequently less efficient [9]. In  conventional  VMD, water vapor drawn under vacuum is typically condensed in a cold trap. Without a proper heat recovery strategy, heat supplied for water evaporation will not be recycled to the process [10]. Gained  output  ratio  (GOR) -defined  as  the  ratio  of  latent  heat  of evaporation to the heat supplied to the process -is commonly used to evaluate  the  energy  efficiency  of  an  evaporation-based  desalination process. For VMD without heat recovery, GOR is approximately one (or slightly less due to heat loss through the membrane) as heat is not being recycled [11]. Despite these shortcomings, insignificant conductive heat loss and relatively high flux are major attributes of this configuration that  make it  appealing given  that  a  proper  heat  recovery  strategy  is devised [12].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several studies have been focusing on improving heat recovery in a multi-stage VMD system via internal and external heat recovery units (HRUs, namely, heat exchangers). Internal heat recovery mechanisms have been employed in the multi-effect distillation (MED), multi-stage flash  (MSF),  and  hybrid  processes,  which  revealed  the  possibility  of achieving a higher GOR value [13,14]. A GOR of 7 -8 was achieved in MED  integrated  with  adsorption  desalination  [15].  Likewise,  such strategies  have  been  employed  for  MD.  For  internal  heat  recovery, Memsys  developed  a  vacuum  multi-effect  membrane  distillation  (VMEMD), which demonstrated a GOR of 2.5 -3 [16]. The increased GOR ( > 1) relied on the internal configuration of a single-effect VMD that consisted  of  a  membrane frame for evaporation and a foil frame for condensation. The foil frame enabled internal latent heat recovery and was used to facilitate the heat exchange between the feed solution and water  vapor  [16].  On  the  other  hand,  external  HRUs  were  used  to enhance  condensation  in  the  multi-stage  VMD  (MSVMD),  and  the resulting GOR was found to be ~4 in a 20-stage VMD system [17,18]. In these  multi-stage  configurations  that  employed  internal  and  external heat  recovery  strategies,  the  vacuum  pressure  of  the  permeate  side decreased  continuously  over  stages  [16,17].  Water  vapor  can  be condensed at reduced pressure in each stage by feed water. The resulting GORs  indicate  that  the  permeate  was  partially  condensed  at  such  a design, which would become poor as a result of the reduced saturation temperature at reduced pressure [18,19].

texts.orig: One  promising  strategy  to  intensify  condensation  of  the  VMD permeate is to increase the pressure of the permeate flow to increase saturation  temperature.  Mechanical  vapor  compression  (MVC)  was proposed to increase the permeate water vapor pressure in V-MEMD and MED [20,21]. Based on the thermodynamic properties of water vapor, saturation temperature increases with vapor pressure increasing; as a result,  vapor  condensation  at  a  higher  temperature  will  be  achieved more easily as the driving force increases for heat to exchange with a colder stream. This approach can make water and heat recovery from VMD more practical. Si et al. experimentally demonstrated the viability of  MVC  for  intensifying  condensation  in  MSVMD  [22].  The  authors established an integrated system in which a steam compressor was used to compress water vapor from the permeate channel and retrieved the latent heat of condensation to preheat the feed solution [22]. Their results suggested that the hybrid process after the start-up could rely on the  compression  work  without  additional  heat  input;  however,  it  is worth noting that the work and heat are different grades of energy [23]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Different approaches have been proposed to take into account the non-ideal behavior of the aqueous electrolyte solution for exergy calculations. Seawater thermodynamic properties correlations were employed  in  the  exergy  analysis  of  various  desalination  processes [28 -30].  However,  this  method  is  only  applicable  to  the  seawater salinity range of 0 -120 g/kg (NaCl solution) and may not be as accurate for reliable extrapolation for other electrolyte solutions and hypersaline systems ( > 120 g/l) [28 -30]. Activity coefficient models have also been developed to capture the non-ideal behavior of the electrolyte solution due to ionic interactions. To date, the most widely applied models are Pitzer  and  electrolyte  NRTL (eNRTL)  models that have proven to be reliable in predicting thermodynamic properties over a wide dissolved solid concentration range [31 -33]. These two models account for the long-range ionic effects using a Pitzer-Debye-Huckel term but consider the short-range interactions differently. Pitzer uses several virial terms with  numerous  adjustable  parameters  that  lack  physical  meaning [34,35]; in contrast, the eNRTL incorporates local composition theories to elaborate the physical interactions for ions with only two adjustable binary interaction parameters [32,36,37]. Apart from some studies that used the Pitzer model in the exergy analysis [18,26], there is little information  on  the  implementation  of  the  eNRTL  model  for  exergy calculation.  Hence,  an  alternative  motivation  of  this  work  is  to demonstrate the use of the eNRTL model for the exergy analysis of the MSVMD-MVC process and compare it against other established exergy model approaches.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This work aims to assess the energetic and exergetic performance of the MSVMD-MVC process. A VMD model that incorporated the eNRTL model  was  developed  in  Aspen  Custom  Modeler.  The  MSVMD-MVC process was established and simulated in Aspen Plus. A comparative energy analysis of the MSVMD and MSVMD-MVC processes was performed  by  examining  the  effect  of  the  compression  ratio  on  several performance indicators such as heat recovery, specific thermal energy consumption (STEC), and SEC. Moreover, exergy destruction, exergy input, exergy output, and exergetic efficiency were evaluated for both MSVMD and MSVMD-MVC. The result of our eNRTL thermodynamic model  was  validated  by  the  Pitzer  model  and  seawater  properties. Lastly, the primary energy analysis was performed to examine the energy consumption of MSVMD-MVC in the power plant-desalination cogeneration scheme.

texts.orig: work. The MSVMD-MVC process was designed for desalinating highsalinity water, where  NaCl  was  considered in the solution for simplicity.  Aspen  Plus  is  a  powerful  simulation  platform  for  various chemical processes and can interconnect with user-defined models [38]. Since VMD is not a standard unit in the software library, we developed the model in Aspen Custom Modeler based on heat and mass transfer involved within VMD [39]. The VMD custom model was embedded in Aspen Plus for simulating the whole membrane process, along with the eNRTL-RK property method that provides thermodynamic and transport properties  [32,40,41].  Stream  information  obtained  from  Aspen  Plus was used for energy and exergy analysis of the hybrid process.

texts.orig: 2.2. MSVMD-MVC process
texts.text: 2.2. MSVMD-MVC process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Process description. Fig. 4 displays the MSVMD process integrated with MVC. A model brine stream of 11.38 wt% NaCl solution is introduced into the membrane feed loop from a makeup reservoir (#1). This is the typical salinity of the produced water found in the Permian Basin [39,40,42]. The inlet feed (#3) and the retentate (#11) are then mixed and heated to the desired temperature via two HRUs (HRU1 and HRU2) and a steam preheater. The MSVMD system consists of 10 modules in parallel and 4 stages in each module. A plate-and-frame configuration is considered,  with  the  channel  length  and  width  of  1.0  m  and  0.6  m, respectively, which gives a total active membrane area of 2.4 m 2 per module. The warm feed (#8) introduced to MSVMD is divided into 10 streams, each being connected to the feed side of a membrane module. The permeate channels are interconnected in parallel, and vacuum is pulled over the permeate channel with the aid of a steam compressor and a vacuum booster. All permeate channels are assumed to share the same vacuum pressure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MVC  is  used  to  facilitate  the  condensation  of  water  vapor  from permeate lines. Fig. 5 depicts that with a certain compression ratio by MVC,  the  increased  vapor  pressure  is  accompanied  by  a  saturation temperature  rise;  consequently,  this  temperature  increase  intensifies heat exchange owing to the improved temperature difference. In addition, water vapor from permeate channels of MSVMD is slightly superheated [17], and it becomes more superheated after being compressed. Heat exchange takes place between the compressed vapor (#17) and brine (#5) in HRU2, along with water vapor condensation enabled by compression. The brine is preheated by the compressed water vapor, which  is  condensed  when  saturation  temperature  is  approached, releasing the latent heat of condensation. The approach temperature is fixed at 2.5 ◦ C in HRUs, which is commonly selected in process simulation and modeling [18]. The selection of the approach temperature plays an important role in the required heat transfer area; however, it was not varied in our analysis because equipment sizing and cost optimization are not within the scope of this work.

texts.orig: Whether or not water vapor can be fully condensed mainly depends on the compression ratio. An extreme compression ratio turns water vapor  into  a  highly  superheated  state,  leading  to  not  only  more compression work but also high cooling demand making condensation even more difficult. A proper compression ratio enables a high latent heat recovery, reducing heat demand. Given that heat and work are different grades of energy, this work aims to see how two forms of energy consumption vary in the MSVMD-MVC process by changing the compression ratio in MVC, which is defined as the ratio of the outlet ( p 17) to the inlet ( p 16) pressures:

texts.orig: where subscripts (16 and 17) denote streams in Fig. 4. The compression ratio equal to 1 indicates that there is no compression applied to water vapor (i.e., standalone MSVMD process). In such a scenario, external heating is required to preheat the brine solution. The hybrid process with a compression ratio > 1 will consume more work but reduce the heating demand.

texts.orig: Products . The  first  product  of  the  proposed  diagram  is  the condensed  permeate  stream  (#29).  There  will  be  some  water  vapor remaining in #19, which will be separated in Sep2 and then condensed by the cooling water (#26, 11.38 wt% NaCl solution at 25 ◦ C) in the water-cooled heat exchanger (WC1). Lastly, the liquid water product (#24) is discharged at 1.013 bar and 25 ◦ C. The second product is the concentrated  brine  blowdown.  To  keep  the  salinity  in  the  feed  loop constant, a portion of the concentrated brine should be blown down (#13). A high blowdown ratio would result in high energy loss from the feed loop. Hence, we considered 10% of the concentrated solution (#10) to be discharged (at 1.013 bar and 25 ◦ C) while 90% was recycled (#11).

texts.text: 2.4. Exergy analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A useful tool to characterize and optimize hybrid processes is exergy analysis. For both heat- and work-based desalination, exergy characterizes the quantity and quality of the energy based on the First Law and Second  Law  of  Thermodynamics,  respectively  [44].  It  suggests  the minimum amount of work for a desalination process and the sources of irreversibility in the process. To evaluate the performance of a hybrid desalination system, it is necessary to calculate the exergetic efficiency of the system using the specific exergy costing (SPECO) approach [45]. For a typical desalination process that involves changes in temperature, pressure, and salt concentration, the exergy rate of each stream can be calculated by breaking down the thermodynamic properties into physical and chemical exergy contributions as:
texts.text: A useful tool to characterize and optimize hybrid processes is exergy analysis. For both heat- and work-based desalination, exergy characterizes the quantity and quality of the energy based on the First Law and Second  Law  of  Thermodynamics,  respectively  [44].  It  suggests  the minimum amount of work for a desalination process and the sources of irreversibility in the process. To evaluate the performance of a hybrid desalination system, it is necessary to calculate the exergetic efficiency of the system using the specific exergy costing (SPECO) approach [45]. For a typical desalination process that involves changes in temperature, pressure, and salt concentration, the exergy rate of each stream can be calculated by breaking down the thermodynamic properties into physical and chemical exergy contributions as:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: E = e ˙ m = ( e Ph + e Ch ) ˙ m (8)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where E is the exergy rate (kW), and ˙ m is the mass flow rate. e , e Ph , and e Ch are  the  specific  exergy,  specific  physical  energy,  and  specific chemical exergy (all in kJ/kg).
texts.text: where E is the exergy rate (kW), and ˙ m is the mass flow rate. e , e Ph , and e Ch are  the  specific  exergy,  specific  physical  energy,  and  specific chemical exergy (all in kJ/kg).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Specific  physical  exergy  ( e Ph )  is  the  maximum  useful  work  determined by differences in temperature and pressure between the thermodynamic state ( T , p ) and the dead state ( T 0, p 0) at constant salinity [46], which can be calculated as:
texts.text: Specific  physical  exergy  ( e Ph )  is  the  maximum  useful  work  determined by differences in temperature and pressure between the thermodynamic state ( T , p ) and the dead state ( T 0, p 0) at constant salinity [46], which can be calculated as:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where  the  subscript  0  denotes  the  dead  state  that  is  in  thermomechanical  equilibrium  with  the  environment  ( T 0 , p 0 ).  This  study considered the dead state at the ambient condition, T 0 = 298.15 K, p 0 = 1.013 bar. The physical exergy for a pure water stream can be calculated using the steam table [43].
texts.text: where  the  subscript  0  denotes  the  dead  state  that  is  in  thermomechanical  equilibrium  with  the  environment  ( T 0 , p 0 ).  This  study considered the dead state at the ambient condition, T 0 = 298.15 K, p 0 = 1.013 bar. The physical exergy for a pure water stream can be calculated using the steam table [43].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Specific  chemical  exergy  relates  the  difference  in  the  chemical composition  of  each  species  (i.e.,  H2O,  Na + ,  and  Cl )  between  the thermodynamic state ( xi ) and the dead state ( xi , 0 ) .  Specific  chemical exergy can be calculated as the difference in chemical potential between the thermodynamic state and the dead state or from the ratio of the activity of the species in the non-ideal mixture as [47]:
texts.text: Specific  chemical  exergy  relates  the  difference  in  the  chemical composition  of  each  species  (i.e.,  H2O,  Na + ,  and  Cl )  between  the thermodynamic state ( xi ) and the dead state ( xi , 0 ) .  Specific  chemical exergy can be calculated as the difference in chemical potential between the thermodynamic state and the dead state or from the ratio of the activity of the species in the non-ideal mixture as [47]:

texts.orig: e Ch = ∑ x i [ μ ( T 0 , p 0 , x i ) μ T 0 , p 0 , x i , 0 )] = RT 0 ∑ x i ln a i a i , 0 (10)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Exergy  calculation  requires  inputs  of  thermodynamic  properties, including specific enthalpy, specific entropy, and activity coefficient. These can be retrieved from various thermodynamic models [26]. For the use of MD in high-salinity desalination, feed brine typically contains a high salt concentration, leading to a non-ideal electrolyte solution. The eNRTL model, seawater properties, and Pitzer model have proven to be reliable in predicting thermodynamic properties over a wide dissolved solid concentration range [30 -33,48]. The eNRTL model is preferred because it uses only two adjustable binary interaction parameters to elaborate the physical interactions between ions in its physics-informed modeling framework [32,36,37]. Hence, we chose the eNRTL thermodynamic model to determine the thermodynamic properties of the NaCl electrolyte solution (TDS > 100 g/L) in exergy calculation [49]. The result of the Pitzer model and seawater thermodynamic properties were used to confirm the efficacy of the eNRTL method in exergy analysis.

The eNRTL model is preferred because it uses only two adjustable binary interaction parameters to elaborate the physical interactions between ions in its physics-informed modeling framework [32,36,37]. Hence, we chose the eNRTL thermodynamic model to determine the thermodynamic properties of the NaCl electrolyte solution (TDS > 100 g/L) in exergy calculation [49]. The result of the Pitzer model and seawater thermodynamic properties were used to confirm the efficacy of the eNRTL method in exergy analysis.

texts.orig: 3. Results and discussion
texts.text: 3. Results and discussion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We performed a comparative analysis of the MSVMD and MSVMDMVC processes in terms of the energetic and exergetic performance. It is demonstrated that the integration of MVC with MSVMD facilitates the condensation  of  water  vapor,  without  the  need  for  an  external condenser. Exergy analysis of the hybrid process was conducted based on the eNRTL model, and the exergetics of MSVMD-MVC was compared against MSVMD.
texts.text: We performed a comparative analysis of the MSVMD and MSVMDMVC processes in terms of the energetic and exergetic performance. It is demonstrated that the integration of MVC with MSVMD facilitates the condensation  of  water  vapor,  without  the  need  for  an  external condenser. Exergy analysis of the hybrid process was conducted based on the eNRTL model, and the exergetics of MSVMD-MVC was compared against MSVMD.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Performance of MSVMD
texts.text: 3.1. Performance of MSVMD

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Energy analysis: We first examined the energetic performance of the MSVMD process at different feed temperatures. Fig. 6 displays the flux and temperature profiles through the membrane module. As the number of  stages  increases,  a  declining  trend  can  be  seen  in  the  flux  of  the membrane and inlet temperature ( T s) in each stage. The temperature drop  is  due  to  water  evaporation  at  the  feed-membrane  interface, meanwhile leading to a reduction in the driving force for mass transfer. The  average  MD  flux  declines  by + 90% when the feed temperature decreases  by  ~10 ◦ C  (an  increase  in  salinity  is  responsible  for < 5% decline in the flux). The flux decline with the feed entering at higher temperature is even larger as the driving force for the mass transfer drops faster. Table 1 summarizes the properties of streams outlined in the MSVMD process, which includes calculated thermodynamic properties. The heat input Q in from the steam heater can be obtained from the enthalpy difference between streams #30 and #31, resulting in STEC equal to 686 kWh/m 3 in MSVMD. This is a typical value for thermal energy input in evaporation-based desalination processes when latent heat recovery is not harvested [50,51]. Table 2 shows the heat recovered in HRUs ( Q HRU,1 and Q HRU,2) and heat and work consumption (STEC and SEC). The result implies that water vapor does not effectively condense in HRUs, as indicated by the entropies and enthalpies of streams #15 and #19.

The result implies that water vapor does not effectively condense in HRUs, as indicated by the entropies and enthalpies of streams #15 and #19.

texts.orig: The total exergy rate was obtained from the summation of physical and chemical exergy, and the results are shown in Fig. 8. To examine the efficacy  of  the  eNRTL  model  in  exergy  calculation,  the  result  of  the eNRTL model was compared to that of the Pitzer model and seawater properties (see details in Section S2 of Supplementary Data). The total exergy rate displays a similar pattern with different approaches. Notable differences in total exergy values between these approaches can be seen in those salt-containing streams (#4 -12), while minor deviations are displayed for pure water streams (#14 -31). These differences are primarily attributed to the deviations in the calculation of thermodynamic properties of saline streams (#4 -12) such as specific entropy. According to the Maxwell relations, entropy of NaCl solution is derived from the partial derivative of the specific Gibbs free energy with respect to temperature  as  s( T , p , x s ) = -gT [52].  All  these  models  are  essentially developed from the Gibbs free energy but handle the non-ideal mixture behavior  of  an  electrolyte  solution  using  different  mathematical  expressions. The seawater properties method contains the saline part of the Gibbs energy to account for the effect of salt on water properties while eNRTL and Pitzer models calculate the excess Gibbs free energy to account for the long- and short-range ionic interactions in the electrolyte solution. Further discussion on the calculation of Gibbs free energy using various models is beyond the scope of this work, and readers can refer to previous reports for more details [32,33].

texts.orig: Despite the differences observed in different models, the main purpose of exergy analysis is to examine the exergy destruction and exergy efficiency of the MSVMD process, as reported in Table 4. The exergy efficiency of 2.42%, 2.38%, and 2.24% was obtained by eNRTL, Pitzer, and  seawater  properties,  respectively.  A  similar  exergy  efficiency (2.30%) was reported by Miladi et al. who performed an exergy analysis of a solar-powered VMD system [53]. Another important finding that stands out is that the total exergy destruction and exergetic efficiency predicted by eNRTL are closer to those predicted by Pitzer, compared to those predicted by seawater properties. This is primarily attributed to the working salinity range for these methods. The eNRTL and Pitzer models are accurate for an aqueous solution of up to 6 M (0.3506 kg/kg) [33,41], while seawater properties are suitable for the range of 0 -0.12 kg/kg, which islower than the highest salinity of 0.1329 kg/kg in this process. Results summarized in Table 4 indicate that the eNRTL model exhibits acceptable performance for exergy calculation.

texts.orig: emphasis on feed temperature of 50 ◦ C throughout our analysis (see results at feed temperatures of 55 and 60 ◦ C in Section S3 of Supplementary Data). Fig. 9 depicts the energetic performance of MSVMDMVC at a feed temperature of 50 ◦ C and varying compression ratios in terms of heat recovered in HRUs, condensation fraction, STEC, and SEC. As shown in Fig. 9a, the amount of heat recovered in HRU1  remains relatively low (~0.30 kW), which is obtained from the sensible heat of superheated water vapor. This value is found to be independent of the increase in the compression ratio. In contrast, a significant amount of heat is exchanged in HRU2 when the compression ratio increases from 1.00  to  2.14.  Such  heat  is  obtained  mainly  from  the  latent  heat  of condensation at higher pressure. The two values differ by two orders of magnitude  primarily  because  compression  facilitates  water  vapor condensation  by  increasing  the  temperature  differences  in  the  heat exchanger. Approximately, 97.4% of water vapor is condensed in HRU2 at the ratio of 2.14, and the recovered latent heat can warm up the feed to the MSVMD (#5) to 50 ◦ C. External heating (denoted by STEC) and total  work  (denoted  by  SEC)  at  different  compression  ratios  are  displayed in Fig. 9b. The STEC decreases from 686 to 0 kWh/m 3 with the compression ratio increasing from 1.00 to 2.14. Besides, the change in the compression ratio results in a large increase in SEC from 0.70 to 49 kWh/m 3 . MVC facilitates water vapor condensation and latent heat recovery at the expense of more compression work. The result from the theoretical analysis suggests that self-heat recuperation can be achieved with MVC. This finding can be confirmed by a recent experimental study by  Si  et  al.  that  demonstrated  the  performance  of  the  pilot-scale MSVMD-MVC  process  and  verified  that  the  process  can  operate without external heating during steady-state operation with the use of MVC [22]. The suction and discharge pressure of the MVC was set at 0.22 and 0.38 bar, which resulted in a compression ratio of 1.73. This compression ratio is different from what we found in our modeling work because  different  operating  conditions  were  selected  for  their  pilot studies.  The  authors  also  reported  that  the  specific  heating  energy consumption, derived from the compression work, was 81.95 kWh/m 3 , which is within the same order of magnitude of SEC reported in this work (49 kWh/m 3 ). Overall, this result confirms the practicality of using MVC in intensifying latent heat recovery for MSVMD.

texts.orig: feed temperatures and feed salinities in Sections S5 and 6 of Supplementary Data). As the compression ratio increases from 1.00 to 1.20, exergetic efficiency drops from 2.42% to 2.29%. The minimum exergetic efficiency  can  be  found  when  the  compression  ratio  is  1.20.  This  is because the small compression ratio applied does not provide a sufficient temperature difference for heat exchange in either of the HRUs but results in a reduction in efficiency as more energy is consumed for the compression. An increasing trend can be seen after the compression ratio exceeds 1.20, indicating that water vapor starts to condense. The increase in the temperature of the saturated water intrinsically increases the  efficiency  of  the  heat  transfer  in  the  HRUs.  Besides,  due  to  the improvement in the latent heat recovery, the saving on external heating improves  the  exergetic  performance.  As  a  result, increasing the compression ratio from 1.20 to 2.14 leads to an improvement in the exergetic efficiency from 2.29% to 6.85%.

texts.orig: To better assess the effect of integrating MSVMD with MVC, Fig. 12 displays a comparative breakdown of the exergy destruction for each unit in the MSVMD and MSVMD-MVC processes. The exergy destruction distribution  in  MSVMD  suggests  that  90.5%  of  exergy  is  lost  in  the heating  and  brine/product  discharge  to  the  environment  while  only 7.64% is destructed in the discharge in MSVMD-MVC. This confirms that the use of MVC assists with recovering more latent heat for the process, leading to less exergy loss to the environment. Thus, the MSVMD-MVC process exhibits a higher exergetic efficiency than MSVMD by replacing inefficient heating with compression work.
texts.text: To better assess the effect of integrating MSVMD with MVC, Fig. 12 displays a comparative breakdown of the exergy destruction for each unit in the MSVMD and MSVMD-MVC processes. The exergy destruction distribution  in  MSVMD  suggests  that  90.5%  of  exergy  is  lost  in  the heating  and  brine/product  discharge  to  the  environment  while  only 7.64% is destructed in the discharge in MSVMD-MVC. This confirms that the use of MVC assists with recovering more latent heat for the process, leading to less exergy loss to the environment. Thus, the MSVMD-MVC process exhibits a higher exergetic efficiency than MSVMD by replacing inefficient heating with compression work.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A summary of exergetic efficiencies of commonly studied desalination technologies is provided in Table 7. RO, MSF, and MED-MVC are among the  commercial  desalination  technologies  with  exergetic  efficiencies in the range of 7.71%-15.7%. It is clear that standalone DCMD cannot compete with these technologies, in terms of the exergetic efficiency. However, our results suggests that integration of MVC with VMD can improve the exergetic efficiency (6.85%). This is primarily due to improved heat integration and reduced heat loss (both latent heat and sensible heat). It is worth noting that the exergetic efficiency of MSVMDMVC was calculated for desalinating hypersaline brine (123 g/L) while exergetic efficiency other technologies were calculated for low salinity

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. Comparative assessment of MSVMD-MVC
texts.text: 3.3. Comparative assessment of MSVMD-MVC

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Currently, MD is on the verge of commercialization but still suffering poor energy efficiency. It seems that MVC could be a promising pathway for improving the energetic and exergetic performance of the MSVMD process. Here, we aim to provide a theoretical analysis for the scaleup of this integrated process in terms of energy consumption when compared to other commercial desalination processes, such as MSF, MED, and RO. Here, evaporation-based processes like MSF and MED are mainly driven by heat, while the pressure-driven RO requires work input. Although RO cannot  be  considered  for  desalination  of  hypersaline  brine,  we  still include it in our analysis for the point of comparison. Certainly, most processes need both work and heat; work is a higher grade of energy relative to heat. It must be noted that when comparing different desalination technologies, ignoring different grades of energy may lead to incorrect  conclusions.  Standard  primary  energy  (SPE)  has  been  proposed  as  a  common  platform  for  the  comparison  of  all  desalination processes  [23].  In  this  framework,  SPE  quantifies  input  energy  for  a combined-cycle power plant to operate a desalination process with two factors CFe and CFth converting work and heat to the SPE input. Table 8 reports the SPE input for MSF, MED, RO, MSVMD, and MSVMD-MVC

Clearly,  MSVMD  and  MSVMD-MVC  show  a  higher  SPE  than MSF,  MED,  and  RO,  indicating  that  the  current  outcome  cannot outperform  commercial  desalination  technologies.  In  fact,  MSVMDMVC consumes more fossil fuel energy (98.6 kWh/m 3 )  than MSVMD (20.8 kWh/m 3 ). This is primarily because electricity is a higher grade of the derived energy than heat. Both MSF and MED are widely accepted to be more efficient at large scales. Hence, although the idea of replacing external heating with compression work might still lack practicalities in terms of energy consumption, it might become an interesting option when small, distributed inland desalination is considered.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Despite  high  energy  consumption,  integrating  inexpensive  renewable  energy  with  the  MSVMD-MVC  process  seems  to  be  a  potential pathway toward its future deployment -aiming to offset its high energy input. Considering the solar and wind energy in the United States displayed in Fig. 13a [57], stranded renewable energy is usually available in remote and semi-arid regions lacking potable water. These areas with abundant  energy  produce  a  significant  amount  of  hypersaline  brine streams  that  can  find  beneficial  use  after  desalination.  For  instance, Permian Basin in Texas and New Mexico generated 90 billion gallons of produced water in 2019 from fracturing distributed shale oil and gas wells (Fig. 13b) [58]. With the rapid increase in water demand, desalination of produced water should become a critical priority to ensure the availability of fresh water in the coming decades [40]. Assuming that current conventional RO is not feasible for desalinating high-salinity brine  and  considering  the  distributed  nature  of  the  produced  water production in small volumes, intensified MD can find a market to play a role in this area. In addition, its flexibility through modularity would make it a promising approach for handling geographically distributed produced water.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusion
texts.text: 4. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we examined the energetic and exergetic performance of a hybrid desalination process that integrates MVC into an MSVMD system. Compared to the MSVMD system that requires a chiller for the condensation of water vapor, the MSVMD-MVC process has the advantage  of  directly  condensing  water  vapor  under  vacuum  when  a compressor  increases  vapor  pressure.  We  developed  the  VMD  model based on the heat and mass transfer mechanism using Aspen Custom Molder and then performed the simulation of the MSVMD and MSVMDMVC process using Aspen Plus. We performed a comparative analysis for these two configurations by examining the effect of the compression ratio on the energetic and exergetic performance, and our findings are reported  in  terms  of  indicators  STEC,  SEC,  and  exergetic  efficiency. Based on the results presented, the important findings are:
